ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a fried squid food product entry; the arrabbiata sauce component is not separately coded. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers cooked squid/cuttlefish. The battered-and-fried preparation with arrabbiata sauce is not a distinct FoodEx2 node; closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226791005 represents squid as a food substance. The fried-battered-with-sauce preparation is not separately coded; closest parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of fried calamari with arrabbiata sauce is approximately 200 g, comprising battered and deep-fried squid rings accompanied by a spicy tomato-based dipping sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Squid, mixed species, cooked, breaded and fried (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB 15150)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for breaded and fried squid (NDB 15150 / FDC 175176) used as the primary nutrient anchor. Values represent the fried calamari component. The arrabbiata sauce contributes additional sodium and minor carbohydrate; a blended estimate (approximately 85% calamari, 15% sauce by weight) was applied to adjust sodium and carbohydrate slightly upward. Energy, protein, fat, and fibre are taken directly from the USDA fried-squid entry per 100 g.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for fried battered calamari with arrabbiata sauce. GI estimated at 40 based on the analogous category of battered and fried fish/seafood products with wheat-flour coating, which fall in the low-to-medium GI range (GI 35–50) per Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. The wheat-flour batter is the primary glycemic driver; protein and fat from squid substantially blunt the glycemic response. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (40 × 22.4) / 100 = 8.96, rounded to 8.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~380 mg per 100 g; ~760 mg per restaurant serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to half a standard serving and request sauce on the side, as the combined sodium from the batter seasoning and arrabbiata sauce can contribute approximately 760 mg sodium per full serving, representing over 30% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined wheat-flour batter contributing moderate carbohydrate load (GL ~9 per serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that the wheat-flour batter raises the glycemic load to approximately 9 per restaurant serving; pairing with non-starchy vegetables and limiting portion to 100–120 g can help moderate postprandial glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying increases total fat and saturated fat; squid is naturally high in cholesterol (~198 mg per 100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease should consume fried calamari occasionally and in small portions, given the elevated cholesterol content from squid and the additional saturated fat introduced by deep-frying in oil; grilled or lightly sautéed squid preparations are preferable alternatives.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Calamari with Arrabbiata Sauce is a popular Italian-Mediterranean seafood dish consisting of squid (calamari) rings and tentacles coated in a seasoned wheat-flour batter and deep-fried until golden and crispy, served alongside a spicy arrabbiata sauce made from tomatoes, garlic, chilli, and olive oil. In Singapore, this dish appears on the menus of Italian restaurants and seafood establishments such as Ricciotti and Riverfront Seafood, typically priced between SGD 17.60 and SGD 23.98 per portion. The texture is characteristically crisp on the outside with a tender, slightly chewy squid interior. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 175 kcal, 15.3 g protein, 7.5 g fat, and 11.2 g carbohydrate, with the batter being the primary carbohydrate source. Squid is a notable source of vitamin B12, selenium, phosphorus, and riboflavin. The dish contains gluten (from batter) and molluscs (allergen). The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 40 (low GI), though the glycemic load per restaurant serving (~200 g) is moderate at approximately 9. Clinically, the sodium content (~380 mg/100 g) warrants attention for individuals with hypertension, and the cholesterol content (~198 mg/100 g) is relevant for cardiovascular risk management. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with gluten or mollusc allergies.
common_user_queries: How many calories in fried calamari?
Is fried squid high in sodium?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from the USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for breaded and fried squid (FDC 175176), which is a well-validated approved source. The arrabbiata sauce component required a minor blended adjustment for sodium and carbohydrate. GI is an analogous estimate from the Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables rather than a direct measurement for this specific dish. No HPB Singapore Foodo or FAO/INFOODS SEA entry exists for this Italian-style preparation. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Cuisine corrected from 'Chinese' (input) to 'Italian-Mediterranean': the dish description (Fried Coated Calamari, Spicy Arrabbiata Sauce) and the serving restaurants (Ricciotti — Italian, Riverfront Seafood — seafood) confirm an Italian-Mediterranean preparation. The input cuisine tag 'Chinese' was a seed-data error. 2. Food group corrected from 'Grains and staples' (input) to 'Fish and seafood', which accurately reflects the primary ingredient (squid). 3. Nutrient values sourced from USDA FoodData Central FDC 175176 (breaded and fried squid, SR Legacy). A blended adjustment was applied for sodium (+15%) and carbohydrate (+5%) to account for the arrabbiata sauce component. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for this food item. 5. GI of 40 is an analogous estimate based on battered and fried seafood products in the Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables; no direct GI measurement for fried calamari with arrabbiata sauce was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. 6. Multilingual names provided for Singaporean context (English, Chinese simplified, Chinese traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as the dish is served in Singapore. 7. Halal status marked true as squid is generally considered halal in mainstream Islamic jurisprudence; however, some schools differ — consumers should verify with the specific restaurant. 8. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all reported from USDA FDC 175176 where available. 9. Aliases consistency verified: all entries in aliases_by_language appear verbatim in the flat aliases list. The hindi aliases_by_language entry 'तली हुई कैलामारी' (without 'अरबियाटा सॉस के साथ') is present in the flat aliases list as 'तली हुई कैलामारी'. All other language entries confirmed consistent.
